planting.note: Plant tubers or rooted cuttings in spring after soil warms to 70°F or above in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; spiny gourd is a dioecious perennial (separate male and female plants needed for fruit) that stores tubers underground through dry season and grows as a tender perennial in zone 9.

directions: Plant tubers 2–3 inches deep in well-drained, moderately fertile soil in full sun; provide strong trellis or fence support; plant both male and female plants (approximately one male per 3–5 females) for fruit set. Momordica dioica (spiny gourd, kantola, or teasle gourd) is an important seasonal vegetable in India, Bangladesh, and Southeast Asia — a perennial climbing vine in the cucurbit family that stores energy in underground tubers through dry or cool seasons and produces vigorous new growth annually. The small, 2-to-3-inch, oval fruits are densely covered in soft spines (the 'spiny gourd' name) and harvested young and green for cooking — their flavor is distinctively bitter-sweet, similar to bitter melon but milder and less intensely bitter, making them more versatile for sautéing, stuffing, curries, and stir-fries. In Indian cuisine, kantola is considered a delicacy and commands premium prices in markets. The plant is dioecious (separate male and female individuals) — an unusual trait in cucurbits — requiring both sexes for fruit production. The tubers can be left in the ground through zone 9 winters if mulched. Spiny gourd is a nutritious vegetable rich in protein, iron, and phytonutrients.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Tropical Cucurbits

planting.note: Direct sow or transplant after last frost in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral, sandy to loamy soil in full sun; kiwano thrives in hot, dry conditions and is more drought-tolerant than common cucumber — plant in zone 9 for summer harvest of the dramatic, spiny, orange-gold fruits.

directions: Direct sow or transplant in warm soil in full sun in well-drained, moderately fertile soil; provide trellis support; kiwano tolerates more drought than most cucurbits once vines are established. Cucumis metuliferus (kiwano or African horned melon) is an extraordinary African cucurbit producing one of the most visually dramatic fruits in horticulture — an oblong, 3-to-5-inch fruit covered in prominent, rigid, pointed spines (horns), ripening from green to brilliant deep orange-gold that looks almost artificial in its intensity. The jelly-like interior flesh is lime-green and gelatinous, with a mild, refreshing flavor combining hints of banana, cucumber, and lime. Kiwano is grown commercially in New Zealand, Kenya, and California for specialty markets and upscale grocery stores where the exotic appearance commands premium prices. The fruit is eaten by scooping out the jelly-like flesh with a spoon, used in tropical fruit salads, blended into smoothies, or used as a dramatic edible vessel for other foods. As an annual vine, it is easy to grow in zone 9 summers — direct sow after the last frost, provide trellis support, and harvest when the skin turns deep orange. The vines are vigorous and productive under heat.
sources: Purdue University — New Crop Resources — Cucumis metuliferus
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Specialty Cucurbits
USDA Agricultural Research Service — Exotic Fruits

planting.note: Direct sow in fall (September–October in zone 9) or early spring in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; achocha is a fast-growing Andean cucurbit that prefers cooler growing conditions than regular cucumbers — it thrives in zone 9's mild fall–spring season and produces prolifically before summer heat sets in.

directions: Direct sow in fall or early spring in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; provide strong trellis — achocha is a vigorous climber. Cyclanthera pedata (achocha, wild cucumber, or stuffing cucumber) is an underappreciated Peruvian and Andean cucurbit vine producing distinctive, hollow, pale-green, cucumber-like fruits 3–4 inches long with a pointed beak and soft, edible seeds inside — the hollow interior makes them naturally adapted for stuffing with meat, rice, and cheese (a traditional Andean dish). The flavor of the raw fruit is mild, slightly bitter, and reminiscent of a blend of cucumber and green pepper; they can be eaten raw in salads, stir-fried, or stuffed and baked. The vine is extremely productive and easy to grow, climbing rapidly with coiled tendrils and producing prolific fruits over a long season in cool to warm conditions. In Andean markets, achocha is a common and affordable everyday vegetable; it is essentially unknown in North American supermarkets but straightforward to grow from seed as a specialty home garden vegetable. In zone 9, the fall-to-spring season is ideal; direct sow in September for a fall-through-spring harvest.
sources: Purdue University — New Crop Resources — Cyclanthera pedata
University of California Cooperative Extension — Specialty Vegetables

common_name: Creeping Tick Trefoil (Beggar Weed, Creeping Tick Clover)

planting.note: Broadcast seeds in spring or early summer in well-drained, sandy to loamy, lean to moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to part shade; creeping tick trefoil is a naturalized warm-season legume that volunteers readily in Gulf Coast lawns, pastures, and disturbed areas, fixing nitrogen and providing ground cover through summer months.

directions: Broadcast seeds in spring in well-drained, lean, sandy to loamy soil in full sun to part shade; established plants spread by creeping stems and self-seeding. Desmodium incanum (creeping tick trefoil or beggar weed) is a low-growing, sprawling, tropical and subtropical legume that has naturalized widely across the Gulf Coastal Plain, Florida, and the Caribbean — commonly seen as a lawn 'weed' in zone 9 but increasingly recognized as a valuable warm-season nitrogen-fixing groundcover and cover crop for challenging sites. The plant produces small, clover-like, three-leaflet leaves on trailing stems and small, pink to lavender pea-like flowers in loose racemes from spring through fall, followed by flat seed pods with small segments that adhere to clothing and fur (the 'beggar weed' or 'tick' name). As a low-input cover crop, it fixes atmospheric nitrogen through root symbiosis, tolerates drought and poor soil, and provides pollinator support with its small flowers. In tropical and subtropical pasture systems, it is used as a legume component of grass-legume mixtures for soil improvement. Note: the sticky seed pods make it unsuitable near recreation areas where clothing contamination is unwanted.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Legumes
Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Warm-Season Legumes

planting.note: Plant bulbs in spring in moist to wet, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral, moderately fertile soil in full sun to part shade; Texas spider lily is native to wet meadows, creek margins, and roadside ditches in south Texas, Louisiana, and the Gulf Coastal Plain and thrives in seasonally moist to wet conditions where many other bulbs fail.

directions: Plant bulbs 4–6 inches deep in moist to wet, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to part shade; Texas spider lily tolerates and even prefers seasonally flooded or consistently wet conditions. Hymenocallis liriosme (Texas spider lily or spring spider lily) is a native Gulf Coastal bulb producing one of the most elegantly beautiful flowers of any native American plant — strikingly large, pure-white flowers with narrow, reflexed, spider-like tepals (petals) 3–4 inches long radiating from a central, cup-shaped corona (like a small daffodil cup), produced on 18-to-30-inch naked stems in March–May before the leaves fully emerge. The fragrance is intensely sweet and vanilla-like, especially in the evening. The plant is native to wet prairies, roadside ditches, creek margins, and moist meadows from south Texas through Louisiana, blooming prolifically in the moist springs of the Gulf Coastal Plain. In garden conditions, it thrives in rain gardens, pond margins, boggy areas, and any consistently moist to seasonally wet location. The broad, strap-shaped, bright-green leaves remain attractive after flowering. Unlike many spider lilies that need dry summers, H. liriosme tolerates summer moisture. An outstanding native bulb for zone 9 gardeners seeking spectacular spring flowers in wet spots.
sources: Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Native Bulbs
